speaker: interviewer
text: Maiden name Katz. Today is January 31st, 1996. My name is Michelle [INAUDIBLE]. We're conducting this interview in Weston, Connecticut and the interview language is English. So where did you go next?

speaker: interviewee
text: After-- as I said, we couldn't stay at Stelo's place-- who, by the way, has a tree at the Yad Vashem because he was a righteous Christian. And he was a fantastic man. The two women, and my sister and I, and a couple of other children, we went from place to place. And I don't remember every place we went to. We ended up in a home-- also in Buda, but don't ask me the address. I have no idea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] A much larger home. There were a lot of children in there, all ages. And I don't know what happened to the adults, and I don't know what happened to the other children. Susan and I were taken into that home. It was a big home and very impersonal, not like the nice place that we were burnt out of. And my memories from that place are very, very bad. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All I remember when I think back is terrible hunger, incredible hunger. We had almost nothing to eat, almost nothing. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Sundays, they had a service. This-- I think it was a Red Cross home. And they had Christian services, of course. And everybody had to take part, whether you liked it or not. But I didn't care. And then after the service, we got like three or four Royal Ann cherries. And that was like heaven. To this day, I love Royal Ann cherries. That was once a week on Sunday, Royal Ann cherries. It was the greatest thing. All week we were starving. It was-- I don't know what we ate. There was no bread. I don't know what we ate. All I remember is that I was constantly hungry. And also after a few days of being there, I started getting lice. And a few days later, they put me in a barber's chair and they shaved my head completely. And I remember looking in the mirror. I didn't recognize myself. And I was crying. They shaved my sister, too. And we looked like two little idiots, two little melons. [LAUGHS] And then we both got sick. I had everything from ringworm to TB-- mild case of TB. And Susan got real sick. Now, I always felt responsible for her because I was the big sister, and also, she was my only family. I mean, I had a reunion with my mother-- a short one, overnight. But who-- I didn't know whether I was going to see her again. So my sister was my only family. And they took her to an infirmary because she had fever, and I was so scared that I'm not going to see her again either. And then I'm all alone in the world. And I-- I remember I used to sneak into the room where they took her just to see if she was still there. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was also sick, but I was in better condition than she was because I was older. And liberation came to us in April in that place because-- although by that time, most everything was liberated. My parents couldn't come for us because there were no bridges still. So the day that they opened the first temporary bridge, my mother and uncle Regeur came to get us. They went to the Red Cross. And again, they were told they don't know where we are. They should just go to all the Red Cross homes and see if we are in any one of them. And they found us. And they were taking us back home, which was great reunion. It was fantastic to see my mother again. And as we were crossing the bridge, at the bridgehead, there were Russians. And the Russians love kids. They just adore kids. They may be raping women, but kids, they love. And they gave each of us a loaf of bread-- my sister one and I got one. My sister ate that bread so fast. She just-- one minute, it was here. One minute, [LAUGHS] it disappeared in that little person. She got so sick that she-- under normal circumstances, she would have had to gone-- be taken to a hospital. But my mother didn't want to take her to the hospital, because she said conditions weren't that great in the hospital. She didn't want her to get something else, too. And-- they took her to a doctor. And somehow they gave her something and she was OK later. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] after that, of course, my mother deloused us and tried to nurse us back to health, which was very difficult because we both had TB. And I'm so surprised. I was so full of ringworm. But by some miracle, I only have one scar [LAUGHS] on my wrist, which isn't a big deal. And at this point, this is 1945. People were trickling back who survived. And the first one who came back was my grandpa on my mother's side, Grandpa Gluck. But he was in such bad condition. He was practically beaten to death. He was paralyzed on one side. He lost the power of speech. And he was so emaciated that when my father's brother brought a loaf of bread, the two of them fought over it. My grandfather was like an animal. He was just so emaciated, so hungry, he would kill for that bread. And I understand from what he could describe to us, that he was living under terrible circumstances in the slave labor camp and had a very cruel officer. And several times, they hung him up with-- upside down with-- by his feet and left him there all night. And some nights, they made him sit up somewhere high and make sounds like a rooster all night. And they beat him very, very frequently, and he got a hemorrhage. And that's when he lost the power of speech. But he came back. So he was the first one. Then one day, we were living at the old apartment-- the same place where we lived before the war. And I was playing in front of the apartment, and my mother's younger brother came into the courtyard. He also came back from slave labor camp. Now he was only 23 years old and he had typhoid fever. And he saw me up on the-- I don't know if you've ever seen how the apartment buildings are built in Europe. They have an inner corridor around a courtyard. He saw me on the inner corridor, and he yelled up to me, and he collapsed. And was-- he almost died of the typhoid fever that he had, but he recovered. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then my aunt-- my other uncle came back from-- my mother's older brother. And his wife-- the one who was in the ghetto with my grandmother first-- she survived with my two cousins. Except that during her time in the ghetto, she gave birth to-- I don't know if it was a boy or girl-- and she had no milk to give the child, and the child died. And my aunt carried the dead child around for I don't know how long. And she-- she went insane. And her insanity manifested itself, among other things, by not eating anything-- starving even when food was available. And my [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] other uncle, my father's older brother who had a child and a wife with my grandmothers who died in Varosmajor, along with the grandmothers. He never recovered from that. He-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] he finally came to this country and he's-- he has a degree in chemical engineering. And he ended up being a busboy even though he spoke English, because he just lost all interest in life.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- but he did totally insane things. Like he-- he found some-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know what Napoleons are? Those gold coins. I don't know. He found a hundred Napoleons some place that Grandma put away somewhere. And he was getting ready to leave the country in 1946, and [LAUGHS] he-- he was going to take it with him. And he went and he melted it down into blocks-- I mean rods. When my father found that, he flipped. He couldn't believe that his brother had gone totally crazy.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, my mother's oldest brother didn't come back from slave labor. His wife and his five children were burnt in Auschwitz. And at that time in 1945, getting food in Hungary-- at least in the city-- was extremely difficult. There was starvation practically. And in order to nurse us back to health, my mother needed to have plenty of food for us.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my-- my uncle whose wife went insane, invited us down to-- again to Kiskoros because his wife's family owned vineyards there. And there was plenty food there. And besides, he had two children and his wife couldn't take care of them. His wife needed taking care of, so he said to my mother, you come down, take care of the kids and my wife, and you'll have plenty of food and everything.

speaker: interviewee
text: And by the way, something that I left out earlier, the entire Katz family had put everything together in a safe-- dollars, gold, diamonds, all kinds of valuables, jewelry. And before the war, a member of the family took it down to Kiskoros to these people who were called Schwartz, who had the vineyards. And they were supposed to bury it underground someplace and keep it for everybody for after the war. Because otherwise, the Germans took everything away or the Hungarians, one or the other-- mostly the Hungarians. Before the Germans got to us, the Hungarians did a good job. Anyway, so everything from the Katz family went down there. Everything from the Schwartz family, everything from the Gluck family. Whatever everybody had, almost everything went there. Except for the money that was in numbered Swiss accounts from my grandmother, but no one knows where it is because my grandmother died. And the money she sent out to this country, to California, which we got when we got to this country. But the stuff that was sent down-- the valuables that were sent down to Kiskoros, we never found. First of all, the Schwartz family-- the entire Schwartz family was taken to Auschwitz, and not one person survived. So there was no one who knew where it was buried. Besides, once we went down to Kiskoros in '46 at my uncle's invitation, we dug up everything, and we couldn't find anything. Couldn't find anything. So maybe somebody found it before us. Anyway, we had food in Kiskoros and things were OK. My aunt died, and we moved back to Budapest. But I wasn't yet quite OK, so the doctor said to my mother that I need to be in a place where there is fresh air. I was five years old and school starts in Hungary at the age of six. And they took me to a place.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a school. Just outside of Budapest, there is a mountain. And on top of the mountain there was a school. Forest school, they called it, because it was in the forest. And they didn't take kids except from 6-- age 6 on. But that we lied. Why not? We lied before. [LAUGHS] So we lied and they took me. And my mother and my father said to me, never mind if you can't learn the stuff. Don't put pressure on yourself. If you learn, you learn. If  you don't learn, you don't learn. You're here to recuperate, which was fine. Except I must have been dyslexic because I had great trouble with the E and the number three. I always wrote them the opposite way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And things were OK, although again, I was alone without my family and only five years old. Things were OK until the first religious instruction. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was the only Jewish girl. And there was one little Jewish boy, also about the same age. And the teacher, the religion teacher started talking about Jesus Christ. And said that, our Lord, Jesus Christ, was murdered by the rotten Jews.

speaker: interviewee
text: And at that point, I got hysterical. It just-- everything just came back to me. Again, I am being blamed for something that happened more than 1,900 years ago. And what did I have to do with it? In fact, what did most of the Jews have to do with it? So I was hysterical. But I couldn't communicate to my parents because there were no phones available to a 5-year-old. And only on Sundays they came to visit. And I told my parents when they came on Sunday, I don't want to stay here. You know that they told me that I killed Christ.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they went to the principal and the principal promised that they're going to get a Jewish religious-- religion instructor. And indeed they did. But once the children found out now that I was going to Jewish instruction, there was no end of it. I killed their Christ. And never mind, nobody learned from the Holocaust. [LAUGHS] It was the same story all over again.

speaker: interviewee
text: So the next Sunday, my parents came. I said, I can't stay here, so they took me back home. And then, being five years old, sometimes I needed entertainment. And my parents decided to take me to the movies one day to see-- I-- I think it was Pinocchio, but I'm not sure because I didn't see the movie. In those days, we didn't have television and we didn't have news programs. So the news was presented before the main feature in the movie theater.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that day, the newsreel was about when the-- when Auschwitz was liberated. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they showed these people who were alive, but looked like dead to me and mounds of corpses. And it had an effect on me. I was so hysterical that in my mind as a five year old, I associated Auschwitz and the movie theater. And I did not set foot in a movie theater until I was a teenager. And by the way, when people came back and they were talking about Auschwitz, and they told me my cousins were burnt in the ovens, I had incredible nightmares. I thought that they put the people in to the flame directly. I thought the gas chambers were something else. Some people went to gas chambers, some people were just thrown into the ovens. So in my mind, I saw my cousins being thrown into the ovens. And then in 1946, I started school-- first grade. And I went to the Jewish school. And I was late every single day because I had such nightmares I couldn't sleep at night. So my parents didn't want to wake me up after an hour of sleep or something like that. So I remember my father taking me to school like at 11 o'clock every day. But they were very understanding at the Jewish school. And since I was a very bright child and I was ahead of the class anyway, they didn't bother me too [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So the nightmares were horrible. I used to dream that they're going to shoot me. They're going to shoot my cousins. They're going to shoot my parents. And every time the shot rang out, I woke up.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I had dreams that weren't as terrible as these, like reminiscent of the fire where I was before in Buda. I would be in a house that's burning and it would be very cold outside. And in Europe, everybody-- every child wears these high shoes laced up. I hated them. [LAUGHS] In my dreams, I would be trying to lace up my shoes and it's difficult. And finally, I lace it up. I go downstairs. I'm in the snow, and I am barefoot. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Those weren't so bad, those dreams. But when I dreamt about the night when I was stumbling on the frozen corpses, those were horrible dreams. And this plagued me until fourth grade. And there were no psychiatrists to help me. And in general, nobody cared. Leave us alone with your problems. Who cares? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And my father was trying to start up the business-- which originally the Germans closed down, then the Russians looted. And so he started up the business, and he was doing OK. But then in 1948, the Communists nationalized it. So first the Germans, then the Russians, now the Communists. And now in 1948, my brother was born. My father was a lifelong Zionist. He always wanted to go to Israel, and he was a member of the Mizrahi. Mizrahi's headquarters were in the building where my grandparents lived. Oh, and by the way, what I forgot to tell you that since my grandparents [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] died, we moved into their place because they had a very big apartment. And my aunt and my cousin-- this is my aunt on my father's side, whose husband was a doctor and didn't come back from slave labor. They lived with us and my father provided for them as well. We had like one third of the floor of an apartment building because you know, that's-- that was Grandma's and Grandpa's place. And we just moved in. Most of the stuff that wasn't looted, their furniture was there. And I guess the people who looted the place did not realize that Meissen china was valuable, so it was still there. And my grandmother was a very fancy lady in her salamander shoes by the bags full [LAUGHS] were there, and fancy hats, and evening gowns. I had a great time as a kid putting them on, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyway, we moved into that place. And as I said, in 1948, my brother was born. We were already living there. And now in 1948, they nationalized the store. So now, my father had no means of making a living. Plus, if you did not hold onto-- if you did not have a job, you were-- how can I translate that? It was punishable by prison if you were a loiterer or I don't know-- whatever you would call somebody who doesn't have a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: Never mind that you didn't-- you weren't on public assistance, which nobody was on. But even if you lived off of your own money, it doesn't matter. It was punishable. It was a punishable crime, punishable by prison if you didn't work. So there's my father. They take his livelihood away, and now they were going to throw in prison because he doesn't have a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went to the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Jewish congregation, and they gave him a job in the [NON-ENGLISH] it was called. And he would be going around to the countryside, all around the countryside, collecting dues from the different villages where some people went and resettled. And that's what he did.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime of course, that's not what we lived on, but whatever was left from the business, whatever they could salvage from it. And in the-- this-- this was in 1948. Now by 1950 or so, communism had become a real menace in Hungary and you had to watch what you were saying. And there were different classes of people in the classless society. There were the people, the undesirables, who were either the ex-nobility or the capitalists-- which we were part of. Or the the intelligentsia, who was everybody who had a high school-- never mind college degree-- a high school degree. And then there were the peasants and the workers. Now the peasants and the workers were the ones who got all the best jobs, the best apartments, et cetera. Those were the ones who could get into the university, even if their grades weren't that good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And [SIGHS] so now again, I found myself at the wrong end of the stick. Because number one, I was a Jew. Number two, I was of the wrong parentage-- capitalists, right? So at that point in 1950s during Stalinism, they started deporting people from the capital city and anybody who was considered to be an enemy of the people. And we were waiting to be deported any minute. Fortunately, it didn't happen. They overlooked us for some reason or maybe there was a reason for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they deported a lot of Jews into the countryside. In to places where there was no running water, no facilities. There wasn't a doctor. There was no way of making a living. The houses were hovels with dirt floors. No medication available, no food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when my father was traveling the country, he saw this, and he felt terrible pity for these people. And he went back to the Jewish congregation and said, at least our brethren we must help. These people are going to die. And if those people were not allowed to go up to the cities or anywhere. I mean, they could go out of their homes, but they weren't able to travel anywhere. And nobody would hire them for anything, so they couldn't make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then he went to his friend who was the Israeli consul at the time in Budapest. His name was-- I think his name was [NON-ENGLISH]. I know I met him. And he told him. And they arranged that through the Israeli consul, they're going to channel money from the US Jewish organizations and from Israel. And my father can take it to those people so they can buy food. And they also [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] brought in Western medications, which my father distributed to whoever needed it. Now in 1952, my father got caught doing this. I don't know. He didn't get caught. That's the wrong word. Somebody denounced him. And one night around 2 o'clock in the morning, they came and they turned the house upside down. And they told my father-- they searched his pockets. And the best thing is that in order to accuse him of all sorts of different crimes, one of these guys was so stupid. He pulled out a Nazi party membership card with my father's name on it, which evidently must have been planted. Because you know that my father was hiding from the Nazis, and not-- he certainly wasn't a Nazi. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they took him, and we didn't know where they took him. We didn't know even if they killed him. We didn't know anything about him. And I was 12 years old at the time. And I remember that I-- I went from prison to prison asking them, please tell me if my father is alive. Please tell me if he is alive. Please tell me if he's here. And I was crying. They couldn't care less. They couldn't care less. And we never knew where he was. And then my mother was worried that they'd come back and search again the house. And we had dollars in the house, which was unlawful. And we had-- having gone through the war, we had all sorts of emergency supplies like sacks of flour and things like that. And so my mother said, we have to get rid of these things. And you know that they're watching the house. Maybe they won't watch you if you go. I can't go.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to-- I was told to whom to take it to, people who were friends and whom we could trust. And what I did, I saw the guy watching me, and he followed me. But I knew the neighborhood better than he did. And there was a block of apartment buildings that had an entrance on two different streets. And when I went in, the guy stayed down on the street watching the entrance, and I went out the other entrance and I went on my merry way and took the stuff wherever I had to take it. And I came back on the same entrance-- same exit that I went in on.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I did this numerous times and he never caught on. So I always looked over my shoulder to see if there's anybody. I-- I learned very quickly and I--
